topic: Why people obey authority even when they should not — Milgram

script.title: Why Your Brain Obeys Even When It Shouldn't

scenes.narration: Sixty-five percent of ordinary people delivered what they believed were lethal electric shocks to a stranger. They weren't monsters. They were just following orders.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir — a large mechanical lever being pulled down by a gloved hand, dramatic shadows on a concrete wall, stark overhead lighting, no faces

scenes.narration: 1962. Yale University. Stanley Milgram wanted to know how far obedience to authority would stretch. What he discovered shook the entire field of psychology.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir — an empty institutional corridor with numbered doors, a single bare light bulb swinging, long shadows stretching down the hallway, ominous atmosphere

scenes.narration: Volunteers were told to administer electric shocks each time someone gave a wrong answer. Each wrong answer meant more voltage. The screams started at 150 volts.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir — a row of switches and dials on a heavy control panel, gloved hands hovering above them, industrial aesthetic, deep contrast shadows

scenes.narration: There were no real shocks. The screams were recorded. But the volunteers didn't know that. Many were visibly trembling. Some wept. Yet they kept going.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir — a telephone receiver lying off the hook on a desk, a single teardrop on polished wood, an empty chair nearby, melancholy lighting

scenes.narration: When the authority figure said 'please continue,' most did. Your brain interprets authority as a signal that someone else holds the moral responsibility. Not you.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir — a towering shadow of a figure in a lab coat cast on a brick wall, a pointing gesture, the shadow looming large, no actual person visible

scenes.narration: This is called the agentic state. You shift from acting as an individual to being an instrument of someone else's will. It happens faster than you think.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir — a marionette puppet hanging from strings against a pitch-dark background, strings disappearing into darkness above, hands limp and lifeless

scenes.narration: Milgram's experiments explain how ordinary soldiers commit atrocities. How employees cover up corporate fraud. How good people do terrible things inside institutions.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir — a stack of official documents with stamps and signatures, a pen lying diagonally across them, harsh directional light creating deep dramatic shadows

scenes.narration: The most chilling part? When you're inside the system, you rarely feel like you're doing anything wrong. You feel like you're just doing your job.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir — an empty institutional chair under a single bright spotlight, leather restraining straps visible, surrounded by absolute impenetrable darkness
